paragraphs.body: Growing up in a family that could be described as the ideal representation of the “American Dream" provided me with a strong foundation. My mom, a nurse, always demonstrated compassion and selflessness in her work. I’ve watched her dedicate herself to her patients, often putting their needs before her own. Her commitment to others has had a profound influence on me, particularly in my decision to pursue a healthcare career. She taught me that helping others, especially in their most vulnerable moments, is one of the most rewarding experiences one can have. My dad, who spent his life working in the steel mill, instilled in me the value of hard work and perseverance. Despite the challenges and long hours associated with the job, he remained committed to his work ethic, providing for our family in ways that will always leave an impression on me. His ability to endure the hardships of physical labor and provide for us is a testament to his resilience and grit. From him, I learned that success does not come easily but is earned through relentless effort and determination. My older brother, who is currently studying law, has been an inspiration to me in many ways. Watching him pursue a career that challenges him intellectually and pushes him toward greatness has taught me the importance of setting high standards for oneself and striving to achieve them. Although our paths are different due to his in law and mine in healthcare, his dedication to his studies and his ambition to make a positive impact in society has motivated me to pursue my own goals with the same level of determination. Despite the strong family values and stability I grew up with, I always found myself yearning for something more. A desire for excitement, the rush of pushing my limits, and the adrenaline that comes with risk seemed to define my ambitions. This desire led me to make a significant change in my life, like moving to Jacksonville University to experience something different. I thought that by leaving my comfort zone and immersing myself in a new environment, I would find the adventure and growth I was seeking.

annotations.text: My dad, who spent his life working in the steel mill,
annotations.comment: I'm married to a former steel mill worker :)

annotations.text: Despite the strong family values and stability I grew up with, I always found myself yearning for something more. A desire for excitement, the rush of pushing my limits, and the adrenaline that comes with risk seemed to define my ambitions.
annotations.comment: Nice job introducing some friction to the essay :)

paragraphs.body: However, after a semester, I returned to Pittsburgh, realizing that the external change I was looking for didn’t fulfill the internal craving I had for more. I was constantly looking for ways to “level up” my life, driven by the idea that better opportunities, more excitement, and more success were out there. Yet, when I returned home, I saw how much people around me, especially on social media, were constantly chasing after more. For example, TikTok, Instagram, and other platforms perpetuate the idea that everyone’s life is constantly getting better and that more followers, more wealth, and more experiences equate to happiness and success. This comparison culture, fueled by the relentless desire for more, began to frustrate me. Social media often sets unrealistic expectations and distorts reality, making it seem like everyone else is living a better life than you. The constant pursuit of external validation made me realize how many people, including myself at one point, forget to appreciate the good things they already have. We are so focused on wanting more and more followers, more experiences, more possessions, that we overlook the simple joys and blessings of our present reality.

annotations.text: Yet, when I returned home, I saw how much people around me, especially on social media, were constantly chasing after more. For example, TikTok, Instagram, and other platforms perpetuate the idea that everyone’s life is constantly getting better and that more followers, more wealth, and more experiences equate to happiness and success. This comparison culture, fueled by the relentless desire for more, began to frustrate me.
annotations.comment: I'm interested in the way you keep asking questions and exploring so many different ways that what you're noticing in the world plays out. This is a great example of "essaying."

paragraphs.body: Coming back home to Pittsburgh gave me the space I needed to reflect on what truly makes me happy. I realized that fulfillment doesn’t come from chasing after the next best thing or constantly comparing yourself to others. True happiness lies in appreciating the people and the life you already have. I didn’t need to move to Jacksonville to find happiness. I already had a loving family, a rewarding job, and the opportunity to make a real difference in the lives of others. Working as a Patient Care Technician (PCT) at West Penn Hospital has been one of the most fulfilling experiences of my life. I’ve discovered that the moments that truly bring me joy come from making others happy and helping them in their times of need. Whether it’s simply putting a smile on a patient’s face or helping them feel comfortable and cared for during their hospital stay, the ability to positively impact someone’s day brings me more fulfillment than anything I could find by seeking out more material things or experiences. It’s clear to me now that the essence of happiness lies not in seeking more but in making the most of what we have, and this has shaped my desire to pursue a career as a Physician Assistant (PA).

annotations.text: Whether it’s simply putting a smile on a patient’s face or helping them feel comfortable and cared for during their hospital stay, the ability to positively impact someone’s day brings me more fulfillment than anything I could find by seeking out more material things or experiences.

paragraphs.body: Furthermore, the idea of skydiving is something that seems risky and adrenaline-fueled, it has become a metaphor for my life. Skydiving, in many ways, reflects the journey I’ve been on because it requires a leap of faith, a willingness to embrace fear, and the courage to face the unknown. It also teaches us that growth often comes from taking risks and facing challenges head-on. Stan Shepherd states, “Skydiving showed me that we all have the ability to take a leap into the unknown, and that it’s often the fear of the unknown that holds us back." This resembles my journey from leaving Pittsburgh for Jacksonville, which was my leap into the unknown. Though it didn’t turn out as I expected, it was an important step in my personal growth. It taught me that fear of change and uncertainty shouldn’t hold us back from exploring new paths, but it’s essential to recognize when the answers we seek are already within us. According to Shepherd, “Through skydiving, I realized that every jump is an opportunity for personal growth and reflection, and every challenge brings its own reward.” This reflects the challenges I’ve faced, both in my decision to leave home and return and in my work as a PCT. Each experience has been an opportunity for growth, and each challenge I’ve encountered has taught me more about what truly matters in life.

annotations.text: Furthermore, the idea of skydiving is something that seems risky and adrenaline-fueled, it has become a metaphor for my life.
annotations.comment: This revision doesn't actually introduce skydiving at all until this moment. So, it feels a bit disconnected to the rest of the essay as a whole.

paragraphs.body: As I reflect on the lessons I’ve learned, I am certain that pursuing a career as a Physician Assistant (PA) is the path that will bring me the fulfillment I seek. The PA role aligns with my values of helping others, and it offers the perfect blend of challenge and reward. Through my experiences at West Penn Hospital and my mother’s influence as a nurse, I’ve come to understand that a career in healthcare is not just about medical knowledge but about making meaningful connections with patients and providing compassionate care during difficult times. Becoming a PA will allow me to continue growing, embrace new challenges, and have a direct impact on the lives of others.

In conclusion, the key to fulfillment is not in the pursuit of more but in embracing what we already have. My family, my work, and my experiences have all taught me that happiness lies in appreciating the simple joys of life and recognizing that the things that matter most are already within our reach. As I pursue my dream of becoming a Physician Assistant, I will carry these lessons with me, knowing that the most meaningful rewards come from helping others, taking risks, and embracing the opportunities for growth that come with each new challenge.

annotations.text: the key to fulfillment is not in the pursuit of more but in embracing what we already have.
